tasks.title: SSD: Market research — who buys school schedule data and at what price?
tasks.description: Research potential buyers for a complete US school schedule database. Theme parks, tourism boards, hospitality chains, retail, travel agencies. What do competitors charge? What format do buyers expect (API, CSV, JSON)? What refresh cadence matters?
tasks.notes: Mr. Slate AM session: Comprehensive market research completed. Document at mr-slate/ssd-market-research.md. Key findings: No direct competitor sells district-level school calendar data. PredictHQ is closest but only state-level. Identified 3 buyer tiers: Tier 1 (parks/hotels/airlines, $10-100K/yr), Tier 2 (retail/tourism boards, $2-10K/yr), Tier 3 (edtech/childcare, $500-2K/yr). Recommended pricing: national data license $15-25K/yr, enterprise $50K+. Year 1 revenue target: $150-500K.

tasks.title: CDR User-Facing Slide Deck — simple explainer for end users
tasks.notes:
Create a simplified, user-friendly slide deck about the Canadian Digital Railway — what it is, how it works, and what a user/community would actually do. Target audience: non-technical (municipal leaders, hospital admins, Indigenous community leaders, university deans).

CONTEXT & INSPIRATION:
- Existing pitch deck (full technical/vision): ~/clawd/projects/cdr-scripts/cdr-pitch.html (1712-line HTML slide deck, bilingual EN/FR, railway-inspired design system)
- Presenter script: ~/clawd/projects/cdr-scripts/script-en.md (slide-by-slide talking points)
- Vision document: ~/clawd/memory/canadian-digital-railway.md (architecture, pricing, governance, competitive landscape)

TECH HEADSTART:
- Reuse the design system from cdr-pitch.html (CSS variables, fonts, railway palette: --rail-charcoal, --maple-red, --accent-gold, etc.)
- Same HTML slide format works well — single-file, self-contained, presentable in browser
- Bilingual toggle pattern already built in the existing deck

KEY DIFFERENCE from the pitch deck:
- The pitch deck sells the VISION to funders/government. This deck explains the EXPERIENCE to end users.
- Focus on: What is a "station"? What does it look like in my building? What can I do with it? How do I get started?
- Keep it to ~8-10 slides max. Plain language. Visual. No jargon.

Source: Fred idea in #canadian-digital-railway (Mar 14).

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Define CDR-OS minimal spec + cdr-strip protocol extraction tool
tasks.notes: Define the minimum viable CDR-OS AND the cdr-strip tool (protocol extraction). Two parts: (1) What software stack does a station need? Local LLM inference (MLX/llama.cpp), Terry agent, VAP type system, protocol packaging, registry client. (2) cdr-strip tool that takes a working Clawdbot/application workspace and produces a shareable VAP protocol package. Test subject: Wilma (Station Zero). Output: spec doc + prototype architecture. Reference: ~/clawd/memory/canadian-digital-railway.md (Layers 2, 2.5, 2.75).

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Build Station 1 — Wilma (Station Zero / proof of concept)
tasks.notes: Wilma IS Station Zero — the proof of concept. Task: package Wilma as a CDR station by applying VAP to extract shareable protocols from the existing workspace. Test: can we produce a protocol package from Wilma that someone else could deploy without any of Fred's data? This replaces the Anik/Bruce pilot as the immediate first test. Skills, cron patterns, agent architecture, workflow templates = the protocol. USER.md, memory, project data = stays private. Depends on: task-063 (spec).

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Build Station 2 — deploy extracted protocol on fresh instance
tasks.notes: Take the protocol package extracted from Wilma (Station Zero) and deploy it on a fresh Clawdbot instance. Can someone spin up a new station, plug in their own USER.md and data, and have a working AI assistant with Wilma's capabilities? Measure: time to deploy, what works out of the box, what needs manual adaptation, quality of Terry's mapping assistance. This is the smoking-gun test. Depends on: task-064 (Station 1 extraction).

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Build Protocol Registry v0 (two-node MVP)
tasks.notes: Build minimal Protocol Registry that works between two stations. Needs: protocol packaging format (schema contract + adaptation guide + code bundle), publish command (Terry-assisted sanitization), discovery (search/browse), deployment (pull + adapt to local data). Does NOT need to be fancy — a git repo with structured metadata would work for v0. The goal is proving the cross-pollination loop, not building a production platform.

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Terry agent — MVP local AI assistant
tasks.notes: Build Terry — the local AI station agent. MVP needs: conversational interface, ability to help users build simple tools/workflows, protocol publishing assistant (sanitization + packaging), protocol deployment assistant (schema mapping + adaptation). Could start as a Clawdbot instance with CDR-specific skills. Reference: Terry section in ~/clawd/memory/canadian-digital-railway.md.

tasks.title: CDR Phase 0: Cross-pollination test — track protocol sharing between stations
tasks.notes: The critical test: can a protocol extracted from one station be discovered and adapted at another? Initial test: Wilma (Station Zero) → fresh instance (Station 2). Track: time to discover, time to adapt, what Terry handled automatically vs what needed human input, quality of the adapted result. Future: extend to real external users (Anik - physio, Bruce - retail were suggested as hypothetical pilots). This is the case study data for funding applications. Depends on: task-064, task-065, task-066.

tasks.title: CDR: Synthetic data generator — AI tool to create safe test data from Protected B descriptions
tasks.notes: Build a tool that generates realistic synthetic data from a Protected B dataset description (schema + type constraints, no actual data). Use case: (1) Protocol demos — show how a tool works with realistic but fake data before deploying on real data. (2) Standalone GC product — every government department needs this for testing, training, and cross-department demos. Input: VAP type definitions or natural language description. Output: synthetic CSV/JSON that matches the types and statistical properties described. Standalone value even without CDR.

tasks.title: CDR: Update pitch deck and user deck with Protocol Registry vision
tasks.notes: Update the existing CDR pitch deck (~/clawd/projects/cdr-scripts/cdr-pitch.html) to prominently feature the Protocol Registry and VAP as the centrepiece value proposition. Lead with the knowledge-sharing problem, not the compute infrastructure. New headline: "Share what you know, keep what you protect." Add slides for: Protocol Registry, Variable Abstraction Protocol, the improvement loop, metadata taxonomy. Also connect to task-062 (user-facing deck). Vision doc updated: ~/clawd/memory/canadian-digital-railway.md.

tasks.title: CDR: Design Variable Abstraction Protocol (VAP) formal spec
tasks.notes: Formalize the Variable Abstraction Protocol. Define: the abstract variable naming convention (Y, X1-Xn), the type hierarchy (CDRField subtypes), documentation tier system (type-only / role / usage guidance), and the verification linting rules. Output: a spec document that could be implemented in any language. Reference: Layer 2.75 in ~/clawd/memory/canadian-digital-railway.md.

tasks.title: CDR: Prototype type system library (Julia or other — see task-075)
tasks.notes: Build a prototype package implementing the CDR field type hierarchy: CDRField, TemporalField, CategoricalField, NumericField, and concrete subtypes (CyclicalTemporal, BoundedNumeric, HierarchicalCategorical, RatioNumeric, etc.). Include: type definitions, constructor helpers, pretty-printing, serialization to/from JSON schema, and auto-generation of documentation tiers. Julia is one candidate language (inspired the design due to parametric types and multiple dispatch), but the implementation language depends on task-075 research — could be Rust, TypeScript, Haskell, or Python. Depends on: task-071 (VAP spec), task-075 (language research).

tasks.title: CDR: Build VAP linter / verification tool
tasks.notes: Build a linting tool that verifies a published CDR protocol is clean. Checks: (1) no strings outside abstract variable vocabulary, (2) no hardcoded values that should be parameters, (3) documentation tiers appropriate for sensitivity class, (4) no leaked comments/paths/system refs. This replaces the harder problem of intelligent sanitization — because VAP is constructive (born clean), verification is just linting. Can be CLI tool or Terry skill. Depends on: task-071 (VAP spec).

tasks.title: CDR: Proof of concept — abstract a real TPCR model using VAP
tasks.notes: Take an actual TPCR model (e.g., wait time prediction) and publish it as a VAP protocol. Real test: can we express the model as Y = f(X1..Xn) with rich type definitions, publish it with zero theme-park-specific references, and have documentation good enough that someone in a completely different domain (e.g., hospital wait times, restaurant wait times) could deploy it? This is the smoking-gun demo for the entire CDR concept. Implementation language TBD per task-075. Depends on: task-071.

tasks.title: CDR: Research programming languages for VAP type system implementation
tasks.notes: Evaluate programming languages for implementing the CDR VAP type system. Julia was raised as inspiration for its excellent type system (parametric types, multiple dispatch, user-defined structures). But don't default to Python either — evaluate properly. Languages to consider: Julia (rich types, scientific ecosystem), Rust (trait system, safety guarantees), TypeScript (branded types, web-native), Haskell (typeclasses, formal verification), Scala (JVM ecosystem), and yes Python (ecosystem size, accessibility). Criteria: expressiveness of type definitions, ease of auto-generating adapters for other languages, Apple Silicon performance, data science ecosystem quality, learning curve for target audience (researchers, data scientists, GC analysts). Output: recommendation doc with pros/cons matrix.

tasks.title: CDR: Design Terry mapping engine — auto-match local data to VAP types
tasks.notes: Design how Terry at a receiving station maps local data columns to a protocol VAP interface. Terry needs to: (1) read the protocol type definitions, (2) scan local data (column types, value ranges, cardinality, distributions), (3) propose mappings ("your staff_count column matches X1: BoundedNumeric, non-negative integer"), (4) handle ambiguous cases (multiple possible matches), (5) validate the mapping works before deployment. This is the UX-critical piece — if mapping is painful, nobody deploys protocols. Depends on: task-071 (VAP spec).

tasks.title: CDR: Create Wilma Station Zero case study document
tasks.notes: Document the full Station Zero extraction test as a case study for funding applications. Covers: what Wilma is, what was extracted, what was protected, the VAP process, Node 2 deployment results, time-to-value metrics. Reference inventory: ~/clawd/projects/cdr-scripts/WILMA-STATION-ZERO-INVENTORY.md. Begin populating after task-064 (extraction) is complete.
